plan.description: The Data Management Plan (DMP) of PANGEA4CalVal (P4CV) project provides the guidelines for the management of the research data generated and/or re-used within the project, including the collection, handling, quality control procedures, archiving and accessibility to the data, during and after the end of the project. The plan is based on the Horizon 2021 DMP template and it follows the FAIR principles for data (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). It is expected to be used as a baseline for a more detailed and extended plan for managing the observations acquired at PANGEA, for Earth observation-based studies and Cal/Val applications in the future.
plan.title: PANGEA4CalVal Data Management Plan

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

referenceField.label: Funding organizations
referenceField.description: Select a funder of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Grants
referenceField.description: Find the grant of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Project
referenceField.description: Projects in Argos are perceived as distinct activities falling under a grant or common activities under different grants in collaborative schemas, eg open call for contributions. Please complete it for the grant associated to your organization if your project falls under this category. In all other cases, please leave blank and it will be autocompleted.

systemField.label: Access Rights
systemField.description: Choose how the DMP is displayed after is published on Zenodo. By choosing Open Access, the DMP will be open on Zenodo after the Publication Date. By choosing Restricted Access, the DMP will be restricted after the publication is made.

referenceField.label: License
referenceField.description: Assign a license to your DMP by selecting the most appropriate from the list.

description.description: The Data Management Plan (DMP) of PANGEA4CalVal (P4CV) project provides the guidelines for the management of the research data generated within the project, including the collection, handling, quality control procedures, archiving and accessibility to the data, during and after the end of the project. The plan is based on the Horizon 2021 DMP template and it follows the FAIR principles for data (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). It is expected to be used as a baseline for a more detailed and extended plan for managing the observations acquired at PANGEA, for Earth observation-based studies and Cal/Val applications in the future.
description.label: Horizon Europe

descriptionTemplate.label: Horizon Europe
descriptionTemplate.description: This is the Argos instance of the Horizon Europe template.

selectData.label: Select from the list of options

fieldSet.title: What kind of research output are you describing?
fieldSet.description:
Research data is information (particularly facts or numbers) collected to be examined and considered, and to serve as a basis for reasoning, discussion or calculation. Open access to research data - the right to access and reuse digital research data under the terms and conditions set out in the Grant Agreement.

Other research outputs refer to outputs that are produced or reused during the research and data management lifecycle. They might be objects, instruments and materials in digital form, such as software, workflows, protocols, models, etc. Equally, in their analog form they might include new materials, antibodies, reagents, samples, etc.

fieldSet.title: Is it physical or digital?

labelData.label: Please specify your answer and state the reasons if re-use of any existing data has been considered but discarded.

fieldSet.title: Are you generating or re-using it?
fieldSet.description:
Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context.

If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc. If you are unsure of the license of a dataset that you are re-using, you may use the License Clearance Tool.

Learn more: https://www.openaire.eu/can-i-reuse-someone-else-research-data.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the type of the described dataset?
fieldSet.description:
The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. 
Primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. 
Secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. 
In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.
For more information, have a look at: https://data.library.virginia.edu/data-management/plan/format-types/

labelData.label: Please specify format

fieldSet.title: What is its format?
fieldSet.description:
Different types of data are acquired, processed and stored (preserved and/or archived) in different ways and can be discipline specific. Many proprietary file formats are “containers” for standard file formats. By packaging them into these containers, a software and/or hardware developer can provide additional functionality, usually by streamlining a process, to analyse data acquired on their platform. However, this has the negative consequence of making these data less interoperable.

You may find some best practices for file formats: https://library.stanford.edu/research/data-management-services/data-best-practices/best-practices-file-formats
For more information about preserving data in appropriate formats: https://www.openaire.eu/data-formats-preservation-guide

labelData.label: Provide the byte size of the dataset.

fieldSet.title: What is its expected size?
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

selectData.label: Reason of new described dataset collection / generation

labelData.label: Please specify your answer by providing more details on your selection

fieldSet.title: Why are you collecting/generating or re-using it?
fieldSet.description: Data collection is usually at the beginning stages of research data management lifecycles to set the background of what is needed (data generation), what is already there (data reuse) and how to best use it to fulfill the project’s objectives (why). Here you may add information about the scope and objectives of your data collection process.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find a comparison of different data management lifecycles here: Plale, Beth & Kouper, Inna. (2017). The Centrality of Data: Data Lifecycle and Data Pipelines. 10.1016/B978-0-12-809715-1.00004-3.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is its origin / provenance?

labelData.label: Please specify your answer by providing more context and clear examples

fieldSet.title: To whom might it be useful ('data utility')?

section.description: Beneficiaries should consider which of the questions pertaining to FAIR data as defined in the HE DMP Template, can apply to the management of other research outputs, and should strive to provide sufficient detail on how their research outputs will be managed and shared, or made available for re-use, in line with the FAIR principles.
section.title: Brief description of the described research output

referenceTypeData.label: Select the journal where the publication can be found
Select the literature repository or the pre-print server where the publication can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output support any scientific publication?
fieldSet.description: Create the links between scientific publications and research data.

labelData.label: Provide URL with data access / availability statement

fieldSet.title: Is there a data availability statement provided along with the publication?

section.description:
Please complete this section for datasets that are important to validate scientific results described in publications.

Note: The European Commission offers Horizon Europe beneficiaries Open Research Europe (ORE), an open access publishing platform with no publishing fees. ORE is offered as an additional publishing option to Horizon Europe beneficiaries. When ORE is the selected publishing venue, all requirements for open access to scientific publications are fulfilled, as ORE deposits publications in the all-purpose repository Zenodo.
section.title: Publications

referenceTypeData.label: Select the data repository where the dataset can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output use or support any published dataset?

labelData.label: Add URL where the software can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output use or support any software?

section.description:
In general terms, your research data should be 'FAIR', that is findable, accessible, interoperable and re-usable. These principles precede implementation choices and do not necessarily suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution. Achieving FAIR data relies equally on the choices you make throughout a research process, but also on providers whose services enable FAIRness of data to be met. 

Learn more about the FAIR principles: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4792175/ 

Learn how you can comply with FAIR principles: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-make-your-data-fair
section.title: Making data and other outputs findable, including provisions for metadata

selectData.label: Please select the used Data Identifier System
Please select the used Researchers' Identifier System
Please select the used Organizations' Identifier System
Please select the used Projects' Identifier System

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What type(s) of persistent identifier(s) are used for the described dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Persistent and/or Permanent Identifiers (PIDs) uniquely identify objects, people, organisations and activities and can ensure that the scientific output is accessible even when the URL of the website has changed. PIDs can be assigned to research outputs including publications, data and software/code. PIDs can also be assigned to researchers, samples, organisations and projects. A PID may be connected to a metadata record describing an item rather than the item itself. PIDs are usually provided by data repositories and other deposit platforms. Re3data includes tags to show which platforms that it indexes assign PIDs to their content.

uploadData.label: If desired, upload an image of the used schema

labelData.label: Provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Will you provide metadata for the described dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. Descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its title, author/creator, abstract, keywords. Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What type(s) of metadata?
fieldSet.description: Please outline what type of metadata will be created and how.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Do the metadata use standardised vocabularies?
fieldSet.description: Standardised vocabularies enable greater interoperability across systems. There are generic and discipline specific metadata standards which have been compiled so as to contain vital information that enable exchange while taking into consideration specificities of work and demands in different disciplines, even in different areas of work within a discipline.

labelData.label: URL where the vocabularies of the data can be found
Briefly describe selected vocabularies application for your needs

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Description of used vocabularies
Are the metadata searchable?
fieldSet.description: Searchable metadata are metadata indexed by search engines and are identifiable by web crawlers.

selectData.label: Metadata are indexed in/ by a

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: How are searchable metadata provided?

labelData.label: Please provide more detail for your answer

fieldSet.title: Are keywords provided in the metadata?
fieldSet.description: Keywords and tags help to optimize the possibility for discovery and then potential re-use of data.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Are metadata harvestable?
fieldSet.description: Usually, this is facilitated by services that apply standard protocols for information exchange. For example, in repositories, metadata exchange is supported by the OAI-PMH protocol.

section.description: Data are findable when described with metadata and vocabularies in a standardized way, assigned Persistent Identifiers (PIDs) and are registered or indexed in a searchable resource.
section.title: Making data findable, including provisions for metadata

section.description: Not all data can be made publicly open, hence data can be FAIR but not open, or open but not FAIR or both FAIR and open. Data are accessible when uploaded in a data repository and retrieved by their PIDs. When data can not be shared openly, metadata should be provided (even when the data are no longer available). In the case of sensitive or personal data, anonymization or pseudonymization and specific access rights can be applied. Where accessing data requires the use of complementary methods or tools, such procedures should be documented.
section.title: Making data and other outputs openly accessible

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert the data repository
Add URL of the system hosting a distribution of a dataset.
Add host description

fieldSet.title: In which repository will the dataset / output be deposited?
fieldSet.description:
A data repository, otherwise known as a data archive, is an online collection of datasets that are described and classified in a standard way that makes data discovery and retrieval easier to be performed by both humans and machines. 

For more information on the processes for datasets, you can have a look at https://www.openaire.eu/rdm-handbook

selectData.label: What processes does it make use of?

options.label: Has an open access content policy
options.value: Has an open access content policy

options.label: Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)
options.value: Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)

options.label: Supports authentication and authorization of users
options.value: Supports authentication and authorization of users

options.label: Has data security mechanisms in place
options.value: Has data security mechanisms in place

fieldSet.title: Is the selected repository a trusted source?
fieldSet.description:
Trusted repositories assume a central role in the Horizon Europe for the deposition of and access to publications and research data. They are: 

  • Certified repositories (e.g. CoreTrustSeal, nestor Seal DIN31644, ISO16363) or disciplinary and domain repositories commonly used and endorsed by the research communities. Such repositories should be recognised internationally.
  • General-purpose repositories or institutional repositories that present the essential characteristics of trusted repositories, i.e.: 
    • display specific characteristics of organisational, technical and procedural quality such as services, mechanisms and/or provisions that are intended to secure the integrity and authenticity of their contents, thus facilitating their use and re-use in the short- and long-term. Trusted repositories have specific provisions in place and offer explicit information online about their policies, which define their services (e.g. acquisition, access, security of content, long-term sustainability of service including funding etc.).
    • provide broad, equitable and ideally open access to content free at the point of use, as appropriate, and respect applicable legal and ethical limitations. They assign persistent unique identifiers to contents (e.g. DOIs, handles, etc.), such that the contents (publications, data and other research outputs) are unequivocally referenced and thus citeable. They ensure that contents are accompanied by metadata sufficiently detailed and of sufficiently high quality to enable discovery, reuse and citation and contain information about provenance and licensing; metadata are machine- actionable and standardized (e.g. Dublin Core, Data Cite etc.) preferably using common non-proprietary formats and following the standards of the respective community the repository serves, where applicable.
    • facilitate mid- and long-term preservation of the deposited material. They have mechanisms or provisions for expert curation and quality assurance for the accuracy and integrity of datasets and metadata, as well as procedures to liaise with depositors where issues are detected. They meet generally accepted international and national criteria for security to prevent unauthorized access and release of content and have different levels of security depending on the sensitivity of the data being deposited to maintain privacy and confidentiality.

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert the name of certification(s)

fieldSet.title: Add certification(s)
fieldSet.description: Repository certified to a standard.

fieldSet.title: Add appropriate arrangements made with the repository(ies) where the described dataset will be deposited
Does the repository(ies) assign datasets / outputs with persistent identifiers?
fieldSet.description: Persistent identifiers (PIDs) are key in ensuring the findability of research outputs, including data. They are globally unique and long-lasting references to digital objects (such as data, publications and other research outputs) or non-digital objects such as researchers, research institutions, grants, etc. Frequently used persistent identifiers include digital object identifiers (DOIs), Handles, and others. For further reading on PID types, please refer to https://www.dpconline.org/handbook/technical-solutions-andtools/persistent-identifiers.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Does the repository(ies) resolve the identifiers to a digital object?
Does the repository support versioning?

labelData.label: Provide your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the described dataset / output title?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: How is the dataset / output shared?
fieldSet.description: Indicates access mode for data. If certain datasets cannot be shared (or need to be shared under restricted access conditions), explain why, clearly separating legal and contractual reasons from intentional restrictions. Note that in multi-beneficiary projects it is also possible for specific beneficiaries to keep their data closed if opening their data goes against their legitimate interests or other constraints as per the Grant Agreement.

fieldSet.title: What is the reason of limiting access to the dataset / output?
fieldSet.description:
Explain why the described dataset or parts of its distribution cannot be shared (or need to be shared under restricted access conditions),clearly separating legal and contractual reasons from intentional restrictions. Note that in multi-beneficiary projects it is also possible for specific beneficiaries to keep their data closed if opening their data goes against their legitimate interests or other constraints as per the Grant Agreement.

Embargo periods are tied to sharing and publishing scientific outputs. An embargo might be applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents). If an embargo is applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), specify why and how long this will apply, bearing in mind that research data should be made available as soon as possible.

labelData.label: Due date of effect of the embargo

fieldSet.title: If an embargo applies, please specify when the dataset / output will be made available.
fieldSet.description: If an embargo is applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), specify why and how long this will apply, bearing in mind that research data should be made available as soon as possible.

referenceTypeData.label: Select EOSC service needed to access the dataset

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add tools names

fieldSet.title: Are there any methods or tools required to access the dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Describe processes needed to query and access data. These might be relevant to SPARQL access points, visualizer software, download, etc. Data access controls, such as passwords or firewalls, should be used to limit access to confidential data and protect them from unauthorised changes.

labelData.label: Briefly explain the method(s) need to be applied for data access
URL where the method can be found

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the method(s) needed to access the dataset / output.

labelData.label: Briefly describe the tool needed to be used for data access
Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add used tool

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the tools needed to access the dataset / output.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Is the described dataset / output supported by a data access committee?
fieldSet.description: Explain if there is a need for a dedicated data access committee to evaluate/approve access requests to personal/sensitive data, etc.

labelData.label: Please provide details on your strategy
Please specify how the identity of the person accessing the data will be ascertained

fieldSet.title: Please specify how the dataset / output will be accessed during and after the project ends

labelData.label: Please specify the time period

fieldSet.title: Please specify how long after the project has ended the dataset / output will be made accessible for
fieldSet.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will you provide metadata even if the described dataset / output can not be openly shared?

labelData.label: Creative Commons Zero (CC0)
Other
Please specify the use of other license

fieldSet.title: Under which license will metadata be provided?
fieldSet.description: According to the Grant Agreement, metadata are made openly available and licensed under a public domain dedication CC0.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Do metadata provide information about how to access the described dataset / output?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will metadata remain available after the dataset / output is no longer available?

section.description: To enhance the findability of research outputs, and their potential reuse, standardised metadata frameworks are essential, ensuring that data and other research outputs are accompanied by rich metadata that provides them with context.
section.title: Metadata

labelData.label: Add vocabulary

fieldSet.title: Does your (meta)data use a controlled vocabulary?
fieldSet.description: Controlled vocabularies provide standard terminology as opposed to keywords or tags used to classify information. Examples: taxonomies, ontologies, thesauri.

labelData.label: Please specify

uploadData.label: If desired, upload an image of the schema created

fieldSet.title: If you created the vocabulary, where can it be found?
fieldSet.description: Consider openly publishing the generated ontologies or vocabularies to allow reusing, refining or extending them.

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add used metadata standard(s)

fieldSet.title: Have you applied a standard schema for your (meta)data?
fieldSet.description: You may browse the Metadata Standards Catalog here: http://rd-alliance.github.io/metadata-directory/

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will you provide a mapping to more commonly used ontologies?

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the methodology followed?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: What community-endorsed interoperability best practices are followed?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Does the described dataset / output provide qualified references with other outputs?
fieldSet.description: A qualified reference is a cross-reference that explains its intent. For example, X is regulator of Y is a much more qualified reference than X is associated with Y, or X see also Y. The goal therefore is to create as many meaningful links as possible between (meta)data resources to enrich the contextual knowledge about the data. (Source: https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/i3-metadata-include-qualified-references-metadata/)

section.description: Data are interoperable, meaning they can be easily understood and shared with other platforms and systems, when they are created using standard vocabularies and include references to other data and metadata.
section.title: Making data and other outputs interoperable

labelData.label: If an embargo applies, please specify when the dataset will be made openly available
Provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What internationally recognised licence will you use for your dataset / output?
fieldSet.description:
There are a number of licenses that can be assigned to your research outputs. There are different licenses for software and for publications and data. For the latter, a common practice is the use of Creative Commons licenses which are machine readable.

For more information on the licences required for data under Horizon Europe, please refer to the AGA (article 17).

You may learn more about how to choose a license here: https://www.openaire.eu/how-do-i-license-my-research-data
You may use the License Clearance Tool (LCT) to check compliance of licenses for derivative works: https://lct.ni4os.eu/

labelData.label: Please specify your answer

fieldSet.title: What reusability and / or reproducibility methods are followed?
fieldSet.description: Provide supplementary material that validate data analysis and facilitate data re-use.

fieldSet.title: Will you provide the described dataset / output in the public domain?

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Do you intend to ensure (re)use by third parties after your project finishes?
fieldSet.description: Despite where data are stored for everyday activities during the project lifetime, there are certain options to choose from data storage that ensures reuse in the log run. Most popular are data archives, otherwise found as repositories. Depending of data volume, archiving might be subject to small fees.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Is provenance well documented?
fieldSet.description: Data provenance, or data lineage, is a type of metadata that tracks digital object's history in time and its relations with other entities. You may also check: https://casrai.org/term/provenance-metadata/

options.label: Set up of scientific and technical committee
options.value: Set up of scientific and technical committee

options.label: Use of tools for automatic checks
options.value: Use of tools for automatic checks

options.label: Consistency verified with data models and standards
options.value: Consistency verified with data models and standards

labelData.label: Provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What documented procedures for quality assurance do you have in place?
fieldSet.description:
Quality assurance procedures are closely related to RDM and are meant to validate that data are clean (no duplicates or inconsistencies), error-free, well structured and represented in analysis. Quality assurance checks can be facilitated by the use of tools or scripts.

To familiarise yourself with the process, you may find a checklist here: https://www.eur.nl/sites/corporate/files/2017-11/Checklist_QA_QC_RDM_0_2_MD_20150129.pdf

section.description: Data can be reused when the conditions about how others can make use of the data are well-described following community-standards and are communicated as specified by the owners. Such information can be found in licenses attributed to data and in references about the data provenance.
section.title: Increasing data and other outputs reuse

labelData.label: Add value
Add name other RDM activity associated with the cost
Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What will be the cost of making the described output FAIR?
fieldSet.description: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them, please check: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: How will this cost be covered?
fieldSet.description: Note that costs related to research data/output management are eligible as part of the Horizon Europe grant (if compliant with the Grant Agreement conditions).

referenceTypeData.label: Search by using ORCIDs of people responsible for data management

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add name
Please specify the role

fieldSet.title: Identify the people who will be responsible and their role(s) in the management of the described output
fieldSet.description:
Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and
stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project for the described output. Follow the research data
management and software management lifecycles to guide your steps in data capture, software development,, metadata production, data quality,
storage and backup, data and software archiving and sharing.

section.description: Data management can be costly, especially when its planning hasn't been sufficient from the very beginning of the research process. Costing of data management includes for example potential use of proprietary services and tools or extra effort needed to perform specific tasks or even to develop tools from scratch.
section.title: Allocation of resources

labelData.label: Please specify your selection

fieldSet.title: What security measures are followed?
fieldSet.description: Describe what provisions are in place for data security.

selectData.label: Select from the list of answers

labelData.label: Please specify your answer

fieldSet.title: What conditions do the security measures meet?
fieldSet.description: Describe data provisions, including data recovery as well as secure storage/archiving and transfer of sensitive data.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: How will you preserve the described dataset / output in the long term?
fieldSet.description: Please describe curation and preservation policies followed for repository content.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answers
Indicate (URL) where a protocol from a meeting with an ethical committee can be found.

fieldSet.title: Are there any ethical or legal issues that can have an impact on sharing the described dataset / output?
Does the described dataset / output contain sensitive information?
Does the described dataset / output contain personal data?

visibilityRule.value: Data accompanied by informed consent statements

options.label: Privacy constraints and applicable ethical norms
options.value: Privacy constraints and applicable ethical norms

options.label: Data accompanied by informed consent statements
options.value: Data accompanied by informed consent statements

selectData.label: Will informed consent for data sharing and long term preservation be included in questionnaires dealing with personal data?

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What are the methods used for processing and accessing sensitive/personal information?
Do you make use of other procedures for data management?
fieldSet.description: Please check: https://www.scienceeurope.org/our-resources/guidance-document-presenting-a-framework-for-discipline-specific-research-data-management, https://www.dtls.nl/fair-data/research-data-management/data-management-knowledge-tools/

labelData.label: Please provide links (URLs) and additional information on your answer

fieldSet.title: Documentation of other procedures

reference.label: Simulations of Spectral Polarimetric Variables measured in rain at W-band
Validation of the Aeolus L2A products with the eVe reference lidar measurements from the ASKOS/JATAC campaign

field.textValue: All datasets, such as those from the ASKOS campaign (DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde) are assigned Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs) upon deposition in trusted repositories (e.g., the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC), zenodo). This ensures findability and citability, aligning with FAIR principles.

The format of the described datasets is predominantly NetCDF (Network Common Data Form), with some data provided in ASCII format:

NetCDF Format: self-describing, machine-independent format widely used in atmospheric and Earth observation sciences, facilitating the exchange and reuse of data. This format is particularly suitable for the complex, multidimensional datasets used in P4CV, and it aligns with the project’s adherence to FAIR principles, supporting platform independence and interoperability.

ASCII Format: ASCII is the format for certain datasets, such as AERONET products, which are expected to range from a few KB in size. ASCII is a simple, text-based format that ensures broad accessibility and readability, making it suitable for smaller, tabular datasets like sun-photometer measurements. However, its use is less prevalent compared to NetCDF for the larger, multidimensional remote sensing data.
All datasets will be openly shared
Data Storage: Τhe trusted repositories Zenodo and EVDC employ encryption and other standard practices, safeguarding data integrity and confidentiality during long-term preservation.

The metadata are designed to be harvestable, facilitated by standard protocols for information exchange, aligning with the FAIR principle of findability. Below is a detailed breakdown of how this is achieved:

Repository Support with OAI-PMH: The DMP specifies that all data and their respective metadata will be deposited in trusted open-access repositories, namely Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC). Zenodo, operated by CERN, explicitly supports the Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting (OAI-PMH), a widely adopted standard protocol that enables metadata exchange between repositories and harvesting services (e.g., search engines like Google Scholar, aggregators like OpenAIRE). This protocol allows metadata—such as dataset descriptions, DOIs (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde), authors, and keywords —to be systematically harvested and indexed. EVDC, as an ESA-managed platform, is also likely to support OAI-PMH or a similar ESA-specific metadata harvesting protocol, given its role in aggregating Earth observation data for validation purposes, ensuring compatibility with international standards.

Standardized Metadata Formats: The metadata are structured using standardized vocabularies, such as ACTRIS naming conventions and CF conventions, which are embedded in formats like NetCDF. This standardization ensures that metadata are machine-readable and compatible with harvesting protocols. The Project Coordination Team (e.g., Eleni Marinou, Alexandra Tsekeri) (Section 5) will input this metadata during deposition, leveraging repository tools that expose it via OAI-PMH endpoints, facilitating automated harvesting by external systems.

Web Integration and Catalogue Support: The DMP mentions a dedicated tab on the P4CV website (https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/, under Material->Datasets) that summarizes datasets with links to repositories. This tab, managed by the Dissemination Manager (e.g., Ioanna Mavropoulou), likely incorporates web standards (e.g., sitemaps, RSS feeds, or schema.org markup) that support metadata harvesting by web crawlers. While not a repository, this catalogue enhances harvestability by providing an additional entry point for search engines to index metadata linked to Zenodo and EVDC.

Implementation and Ongoing Process: The harvesting process is initiated as data are deposited, with repositories automatically exposing metadata via OAI-PMH. The DMP’s preliminary nature means that refinements (e.g., additional protocols) may be detailed in the D1.6 update, but the current strategy ensures immediate harvestability. The use of DOIs and standardized formats further supports this by providing persistent, crawlable references.

For data deposited in the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC), the metadata adheres to EVDC’s requirements, which are based on the CF conventions and the GEOMS (Global Earth Observation Metadata Standard) framework. This schema provides a structured format for Earth observation data, including spatial and temporal metadata, enhancing compatibility with ESA’s ecosystem.

Zenodo, another repository used by P4CV, employs the Dublin Core schema as its baseline metadata standard. The DMP specifies that metadata will include elements like dataset description, authors, DOIs, and keywords, which align with Dublin Core’s core fields (e.g., title, creator, identifier), adapted for scientific data deposition.
Legal Issues: All data are planned to be publicly available under the CC BY 4.0 license.

Researchers: The P4CV datase provides processed data for atmospheric studies. Researchers can use these to analyze aerosol and cloud properties, along with their radiative properties, advancing scientific understanding. For instance, lidar data from PANGEA could support peer-reviewed studies on tropospheric composition.

Research Community: The data’s deposition in trusted repositories with DOIs enables broad access for universities, research institutes, and ESFRI projects like ACTRIS and ICOS. 

Decision Makers: Satellite and ground-based data can inform policy on climate change and air quality. For example, Greek government officials might use this data to assess wildfire smoke impacts, to guide environmental regulations.

Education: The dataset supports training activities (WP2, WP3), with ASKOS campaign data available for student exercises. Universities could integrate these into curricula, teaching remote sensing techniques using real-world observations.

Industry: Local SMEs and the space sector (e.g., Raymetrics S.A.) can exploit the data for developing atmospheric monitoring tools. 

The Public: Open access of data via the P4CV website, along with related descriptions at P4CV social media news, allow society to understand climate issues. For instance, visualizations of wildfire smoke spread (as captured on 2 July 2025 at PANGEA) could raise awareness about environmental health risks.

List of Keywords:

  • Remote sensing: This keyword reflects the project’s core methodology, encompassing the use of ground-based (e.g., PollyXT lidar, CIMEL sun-photometers) and satellite-based (e.g., Aeolus, EarthCARE) instruments to observe atmospheric phenomena. It aids researchers and stakeholders in locating relevant Earth observation datasets.

  • Ground-based: Highlights data collected at the PANGEA observatory (e.g., lidar profiles) and at MINDELO station during the ASKOS ESA Cal/Val campaign, distinguishing it from airborne or satellite data and supporting targeted searches by ground-based research communities.

  • Satellite: Indicates data from satellite missions like Aeolus.

  • Simulation: Refers to simulated scattering properties of rain particles, useful for cloud and percipitation remote sensing community.

  • Cal/Val (Calibration/Validation): Directly ties to the project’s WP4 objectives, making the dataset relevant to scientists and agencies working on satellite product Cal/Val, enhancing its reuse potential.

  • Lidar: Specifies the use of lidar technologies (e.g., PollyXT, eVe), aiding specialists in lidar-based atmospheric research to locate these datasets.

  • Radar: Indicates radar data (e.g., acquired from ACTIRS/Cloudnet), supporting discovery by researchers focusing on radar remote sensing applications.

  • Sun-photometer: Points to AERONET aerosol products, enabling aerosol and radiation scientists to find relevant data.

  • Radiation: Covers pyranometer GHI measurements for radiation-related studies.

The long-term accessibility of the dataset following the project’s conclusion is provided, ensuring compliance with FAIR principles and Horizon Europe requirements. 

Long-Term Preservation Commitment: The DMP specifies that data and metadata will be deposited in trusted open-access repositories, namely Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC). Zenodo, as a key repository, commits to retaining deposited content for the lifetime of the repository, which is defined as at least 20 years according to its general policies. This ensures that datasets, such as those from the ASKOS campaign (DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde) and PANGEA observations, remain accessible post-project.

EVDC Longevity: The EVDC, managed by ESA, is designed for long-term archiving of atmospheric validation data. While the DMP does not provide an exact timeframe for EVDC, ESA’s commitment to maintaining its Earth observation infrastructure suggests a similar or longer preservation period, likely aligned with international standards (e.g., 20-50 years), given its role in supporting missions like EarthCARE. The collaboration with ACTRIS further reinforces this longevity.

Post-Project Accessibility: Focusing on open access and the assignment of persistent identifiers (PIDs) like DOIs ensures that data remain retrievable via repository interfaces and the P4CV website after the project ends. For restricted data, metadata will be available, with access conditions detailed in the D1.6 update, but the 20-year minimum applies to openly accessible data.

The Project Coordination Team (e.g., Eleni Marinou, Alexandra Tsekeri) and Dissemination Manager (e.g., Ioanna Mavropoulou) will oversee deposition to ensure long-term availability, with the 20-year baseline set by Zenodo as the minimum standard. Any extension beyond this will depend on EVDC policies and future funding.

Origin of data

  • Data Generated Within the Project:
Simulated data: the simulated scattering properties of rain particles in Tsikoudi et al. (2025).

  • Data Reused from External Sources:
PANGEA Observatory Data Center: Ground-based remote sensing data, including PollyXT lidar profiles (backscatter at 355, 532, and 1064 nm, extinction coefficients at 355 and 532 nm, VLDR and PLDR at 355 and 532 nm), CIMEL sun-photometer products (via AERONET), were collected at the PANGEA observatory. These datasets are used for the Cal/Val activities of P4CV and are publicly available at ESA/EVDC https://evdc.esa.int/ ("Search and Download Cal/Val Data Files" tab).

ESA/ASKOS Campaign: Ground-based remote sensing data, such as PollyXT lidar data, eVe lidar data (backscatter and extinction profiles, VLDR and PLDR at 355 nm), are reused from the ASKOS ESA Cal/Val campaign. These are available under DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde in the online repository ESA/EVDC at https://evdc.esa.int/publications/askos-campaign-dataset/.

ESA Satellite Missions: Satellite remote sensing data, including Aeolus L2A products, are reused from ESA. The Aeolus satellite products are publicly available by ESA Earth Explorer Program, at the online Aeolus Data Dissemination Facility https://aeolus-ds.eo.esa.int/oads/access/collection/Level_2A_aerosol_cloud_optical_products_Reprocessed.


Provenance of data
  • Data Generated Within the Project:
Simulated data: the provenance is fully described in Tsikoudi et al. (2025), and will be also included in at https://github.com/Ioanna-Tsik and zenodo upon acceptance of the manuscript.

  • Data Reused from External Sources:
The provenance is well-documented by ESA providing the data.
The size of the data per file ranges from a few KB (e.g. AERONET products provided in ASCII files) to a few hundred MB (e.g. Aeolus satellite files)

The selected repositories—Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC)—assign persistent identifiers (PIDs), specifically Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs), to datasets and outputs. These repositories are designed to resolve these identifiers to the corresponding digital objects, ensuring accessibility and findability as required by FAIR principles. 

Zenodo:
The DMP confirms that Zenodo assigns DOIs to deposited datasets (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde for ASKOS data). Zenodo, operated by CERN in collaboration with DataCite, resolves these DOIs to the specific digital objects—such as NetCDF or ASCII files containing lidar profiles, sun-photometer data, or EarthCARE simulated scenes—by redirecting users to the dataset’s landing page. This page provides access to the data file, associated metadata, and licensing information (e.g., CC BY 4.0). The resolution process is automated via the Handle System, a global PID infrastructure, ensuring that the DOI consistently links to the digital object even if the repository’s URL changes.

ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC):
The DMP states that EVDC will also assign DOIs to datasets, as seen with the linkage to ASKOS campaign data. As an ESA-managed repository, EVDC resolves these DOIs to digital objects, such as satellite validation data (e.g., Aeolus L2A products, EarthCARE L1/L2 files), by directing users to a landing page or data access portal. This resolution is supported by ESA’s data management infrastructure, which integrates with international PID systems (e.g., DataCite or ESA’s own DOI service), ensuring the digital object (e.g., a NetCDF file) is retrievable along with its metadata. The collaboration with ACTRIS further reinforces this capability for atmospheric data.

The Project Coordination Team (e.g., Eleni Marinou, Alexandra Tsekeri) will coordinate with Zenodo and EVDC to ensure DOIs are assigned and resolved during deposition. Arrangements include using repository APIs to link PIDs to digital objects, with the Dissemination Manager (e.g., Ioanna Mavropoulou) overseeing website integration (https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/, Material->Datasets). The DMP’s “as open as possible, as closed as necessary” policy ensures that even for restricted data, metadata with resolvable PIDs will be provided, though the digital object itself may be access-controlled.

The resolution capability aligns with the DMP’s use of DOIs as persistent, globally unique references, meeting the requirements outlined at https://www.dpconline.org/handbook/technical-solutions-and-tools/persistent-identifiers. This ensures that users can reliably access the digital objects associated with P4CV datasets, supporting reuse and citation. In summary, both Zenodo and EVDC resolve DOIs to digital objects, providing a robust link between identifiers and the underlying data files, as facilitated by the project’s deposition arrangements.
https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/materials_datasets_algorithms.php

The dataset from the Pangea4CalVal (P4CV) project is primarily accessed through standard repository interfaces provided by Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC). Users can query and access data using the following processes:

Download Process: Data and metadata are openly accessible via these repositories, where users can download files (e.g., NetCDF, ASCII) directly using persistent identifiers (PIDs) like DOIs (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde). The P4CV website (https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/, Material->Datasets) provides links to these repositories, allowing users to locate and download datasets with a simple click or URL navigation.

Querying: Repositories like Zenodo offer search functionalities based on metadata (e.g., keywords, authors), enabling users to query datasets via their web interfaces. 

Data Access Controls: For data that cannot be shared openly due to confidentiality (e.g., legal or contractual restrictions), access controls such as passwords or firewalls will be implemented. The “as open as possible, as closed as necessary” policy ensures that confidential data are protected from unauthorized changes, with metadata provided for non-accessible datasets. Specific access rights or authentication methods will be documented in the D1.6 update if needed, but no such controls are currently specified for the majority of open data.

Implementation: The Project Coordination Team will ensure data are uploaded with accessible metadata, while restricted data access will be managed by the Dissemination Manager, potentially involving secure portals or authentication systems as arranged with the repositories.
All data used herein are secondary data: they have been cleaned up, analysed and pre-processed by others and/or the NOA team.

textListValues.textListValue: Has an open access content policy
Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)
Supports authentication and authorization of users
Has data security mechanisms in place

field.textValue: No embargo applies to the dataset/output.

Data and metadata are deposited in trusted repositories, Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC), with a commitment to long-term preservation. Zenodo guarantees retention for at least 20 years, while EVDC, backed by ESA, is expected to maintain data similarly. For datasets that become unavailable (e.g., due to withdrawal or restrictions), metadata will remain in these repositories, linked by persistent identifiers (PIDs) like DOIs (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde), ensuring continued accessibility.

Tsikoudi et al., 2025, [preprint], https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2024-3164
"...the spectra presented in this study will be published at https://github.com/Ioanna-Tsik upon acceptance of the manuscript."

Paschou et al., 2025, [preprint], https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2025-1152
"The reprocessed Aeolus L2A products with Baseline 16 are provided by the European Space Agency (ESA) Earth Explorer Program and are available from the online Aeolus Data Dissemination Facility (https://aeolus-ds.eo.esa.int/oads/access/collection/Level_2A_aerosol_cloud_optical_products_Reprocessed). The eVe dataset is publicly available through the ASKOS repository in the EVDC data portal (https://evdc.esa.int/publications/askos-campaign-dataset/)."
P4CV Remote Sensing and Calibration/Validation Dataset

Descriptive Metadata:
This type will include key aspects to enable discovery and identification, such as dataset description, deposit date, author(s) and keywords (e.g., Remote sensing, Lidar, Cal/Val).

Administrative Metadata:
This type covers technical and management information, such as file format (e.g., NetCDF, ASCII), deposit date, licensing terms (CC BY 4.0), and quality control procedures following ACTRIS standards. 

Structural Metadata:
This type describes the organization and relationships within the dataset, such as the hierarchical structure of NetCDF files (e.g., variables like backscatter and extinction coefficients). It ensures users understand data composition and interoperability.

Legal Metadata:
This type includes licensing information (CC BY 4.0) and access rights.

P4CV has a clear commitment to facilitating long-term reuse of the dataset and outputs by third parties after the project concludes, aligning with FAIR principles and Horizon Europe requirements. 

Long-Term Storage in Repositories: The data and metadata will be deposited in trusted open-access repositories, namely Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC), which are designed for long-term preservation. Zenodo commits to retaining content for at least 20 years, while EVDC, backed by ESA, is expected to maintain data for a similar or longer period. 

Open Access and Reuse Policy: The DMP follows the “as open as possible, as closed as necessary” principle, with most data made immediately accessible via persistent identifiers (PIDs) like DOIs (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde). The use of the Creative Commons Attribution International Public License (CC BY 4.0) allows third parties to use, distribute, and build upon the data with attribution, encouraging reuse by researchers, space agencies, academia, industry, and the public.

Metadata and Discoverability: Metadata, including keywords (e.g., Remote sensing, Lidar) and DOIs, will remain available, ensuring third parties can discover and request access to non-open datasets. The P4CV website will continue to link to repositories, enhancing long-term findability.

Data deposited in the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) will adhere to EVDC’s metadata requirements, which are based on the Climate and Forecast (CF) conventions and the GEOMS (Global Earth Observation Metadata Standard) framework. These provide a controlled vocabulary tailored for Earth observation and validation data, such as Aeolus L2A products, facilitating exchange with other systems.

The inclusion of specific keywords (e.g., Remote sensing, Lidar, Cal/Val) in metadata goes beyond simple tags, as they are informed by these controlled vocabularies.  This use of controlled vocabularies is supported by the adoption of NetCDF and ASCII formats, which rely on defined metadata schemas to ensure interoperability. The project team will implement these standards during metadata creation and deposition, enhancing data sharing across platforms.

All data deposited in trusted open-access repositories (e.g., the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center [EVDC], zenodo) and are accompanied by respective metadata, which includes essential descriptive information to facilitate discovery and identification. 

Specifically, the metadata will cover:
Dataset Details: Description of the dataset, date of deposit, and author(s) 

Licensing Terms: Information on the Creative Commons Attribution International Public Licence (CC BY 4.0) , ensuring usage rights are clear

Persistent Identifiers: Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs) assigned to each dataset 

Authors and Organizations: Identification of individuals and institutions involved in the action 

Data Keywords: Relevant terms such as Remote sensing, Ground-based, Satellite, Simulation, Cal/Val, Lidar, Radar, Sun-photometer, and Radiation, enabling searchable categorization

This metadata will be provided in a standardized, machine-readable format to support discovery, as evidenced by the integration with repositories like EVDC and zenodo, which index data using such metadata. The dedicated tab on the P4CV website (https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/, under Material->Datasets) will further summarize datasets with linked metadata, enhancing accessibility.

The dataset from the Pangea4CalVal (P4CV) project is shared as open. Immediate open access via Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) is highlighted, where data can be retrieved using persistent identifiers like DOIs. This approach targets a wide audience, including researchers, space agencies, and the public. While some data may be restricted due to confidentiality, legal, or contractual reasons, these exceptions are not the default and will be justified later. The focus on open sharing reflects the project’s commitment to broad accessibility.

Access Mechanism: Access is facilitated by visiting https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/materials_datasets_algorithms.php, which directs users to Zenodo and EVDC for downloads using DOIs (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde).
The cost of making the described output FAIR for the P4CV project will be managed with minimal direct costs, relying on existing resources and free repository services.
Direct cost

Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) are identified as the repositories for depositing the dataset and outputs. Both meet the criteria for trusted repositories under Horizon Europe guidelines, and specific arrangements have been made to ensure effective deposition and management. Below are the details, including the appropriate arrangements:

Zenodo:
Certification and Recognition: Zenodo is certified with the CoreTrustSeal, an internationally recognized standard, validating its quality as a general-purpose repository operated by CERN with OpenAIRE. It is widely endorsed by the research community.
Quality Characteristics: Zenodo provides explicit online policies detailing acquisition (user uploads), access, and security (encrypted storage), with long-term sustainability ensured by CERN funding and a commitment to preserve content for at least 20 years.
Access and Identifiers: It offers broad, equitable open access free at the point of use, adhering to the “as open as possible, as closed as necessary” principle, and assigns persistent DOIs (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde) for citation. Metadata are machine-actionable, using Dublin Core and DataCite standards, with provenance and licensing (e.g., CC BY 4.0).
Preservation and Security: Zenodo facilitates mid- and long-term preservation with expert curation and quality assurance, liaising with depositors (e.g., P4CV team) when issues arise. It meets international security criteria with encrypted storage and access controls.
Arrangements: The Project Coordination Team (e.g., Eleni Marinou, Alexandra Tsekeri) will coordinate with Zenodo to deposit data and metadata, ensuring compliance with its submission guidelines. Agreements include using OAI-PMH for metadata harvesting, assigning DOIs upon upload, and adhering to the CC BY 4.0 license. The team will also ensure metadata aligns with Zenodo’s Dublin Core schema, facilitating seamless integration.

ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC):
Certification and Recognition: While not explicitly certified (e.g., CoreTrustSeal, ISO 16363) in the DMP, EVDC is a disciplinary repository managed by ESA, a globally recognized authority. Its alignment with ESA’s standards (e.g., ECSS, influenced by ISO 16363) suggests trustworthiness, pending confirmation via https://evdc.esa.int/documentation.
Quality Characteristics: EVDC offers acquisition services for correlative data, access via DOIs, and security measures outlined in ESA protocols, with long-term sustainability through ESA funding.
Access and Identifiers: It provides open access where applicable, assigning DOIs (e.g., linked to ASKOS data), with metadata adhering to CF conventions and GEOMS standards.
Preservation and Security: EVDC ensures mid- and long-term preservation through expert curation and quality assurance, collaborating with ACTRIS. It implements security levels for sensitive data, respecting legal and ethical limitations.
Arrangements: The P4CV team, particularly through collaboration with ACTRIS central data facilities, has arranged to link PANGEA data to EVDC for archiving and validation purposes. The Project Coordination Team will work with EVDC to submit metadata and data (e.g., EarthCARE products) in CF-compliant NetCDF formats, ensuring DOIs are assigned. Agreements include metadata alignment with EVDC’s GEOMS standard and provisions for restricted data metadata under the “as open as possible” policy (Section 3.2.1), with updates tracked in D1.6.
Alignment with DMP: Both repositories support the DMP’s open access strategy, use non-proprietary formats, and provide metadata for restricted data, meeting Horizon Europe criteria. The arrangements ensure seamless deposition and accessibility.

Zenodo and EVDC are trusted repositories, with Zenodo’s CoreTrustSeal and EVDC’s ESA-backed standards, supported by specific arrangements for data management and metadata provision, fully aligning with P4CV’s needs.

During the Project:

Repository Authentication: For open access data deposited in Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC), users can download datasets (e.g., via DOIs like DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde) without mandatory identity verification, as access is public (Section 3.2). However, Zenodo and EVDC may require users to create accounts or log in with institutional credentials (e.g., via ORCID or ESA login) to track usage or request bulk downloads, indirectly ascertaining identity through registration.

Internal Access: For interim data on NOA servers (Section 6), the Project Coordination Team (e.g., Eleni Marinou, Alexandra Tsekeri) and WP leaders will use internal authentication (e.g., NOA network credentials) to access unprocessed data, ensuring identity is verified through organizational security protocols.

After the Project Ends:

Open Access Data: Post-project, open access via Zenodo and EVDC will continue without requiring identity verification for public downloads, relying on PIDs for traceability. However, repositories may log IP addresses or account details (if registered) to monitor access, providing a basic identity trace.

Restricted Access Data: For datasets that cannot be shared openly due to confidentiality, legal, contractual, or legitimate interest constraints, identity verification will be required.

This will involve:
Authentication Mechanisms: Users may need to register with the repositories or the P4CV team (e.g., via email to imavropoulou@noa.gr) and provide institutional affiliation or credentials to gain access. EVDC may use ESA’s authentication system (e.g., ESA Single Sign-On), while Zenodo could leverage ORCID or institutional logins.

Access Control Tools: Password-protected portals or firewall-secured interfaces will be implemented, as noted in access tool discussions. The Dissemination Manager (e.g., Ioanna Mavropoulou) will oversee access requests, verifying identities against a pre-approved list or through a formal application process, with details to be documented in the D1.6 update.

Metadata Access: For restricted data, metadata will be openly available without identity checks, but accessing the data itself will require verified identity to prevent unauthorized changes or use.
Implementation:

The mechanisms for accessing the dataset during its lifecycle and post-project, ensuring compliance with FAIR principles and Horizon Europe requirements:

During the Project:

Open Access via Repositories: Data and metadata will be deposited in Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) as they are produced, providing immediate open access (Section 3.2). Users can access datasets using persistent identifiers (PIDs) like DOIs (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde) via repository web interfaces. The P4CV website will serve as a portal, linking to these repositories for real-time access to ground-based data (e.g., PollyXT lidar), satellite data (e.g., Aeolus L2A), and simulated scenes (e.g., EarthCARE) for training (WP2, WP3) and calibration/validation (WP4).

Internal Access: Project team members (e.g., WP leaders, Project Coordination Team) will have early access to unprocessed or interim data stored on NOA servers (Section 6) for analysis and quality control, managed by the Project Coordination Team (e.g., Eleni Marinou, Alexandra Tsekeri) and Dissemination Manager (e.g., Ioanna Mavropoulou).

After the Project Ends:

Continued Open Access: Post-project, the data will remain accessible in Zenodo and EVDC, with a commitment to long-term preservation (at least 20 years for Zenodo) (Section 3.4). DOIs will ensure perpetual citability, allowing users to download files (e.g., NetCDF, ASCII) and metadata, supporting reuse by researchers, space agencies, and the public (Section 2).

Restricted Access for Confidential Data: For datasets that cannot be shared openly due to confidentiality, legal, contractual, or legitimate interest constraints (Section 3.2.1), access will be restricted. Metadata will be provided in the repositories, with specific access rights (e.g., passwords, firewalls) documented in the D1.6 update. Users may need to request access through the P4CV team or repository administrators, with justifications for restrictions detailed post-project.

Website Maintenance: The P4CV website will continue to host the dataset summary tab, maintained by the Dissemination Manager, ensuring links to repositories remain functional, though updates may cease after the project ends unless funded separately.

Access Mechanisms:

During the project, access is facilitated by repository downloads and internal server access for the team. After the project, access relies on repository persistence, with no specific complementary tools mandated (though optional software like Panoply may be used). The “as open as possible, as closed as necessary” policy ensures broad availability, with restricted data handled case-by-case.

• Reused Data: Existing datasets, such as satellite remote sensing data from ESA (e.g., Aeolus L2A products), as well as data from the ASKOS ESA Cal/Val campaign, and ground-based remote sensing data used for Cal/Val of ESA satellite missions.
  • Generated Data: Simulated scattering properties of rain particles in Tsikoudi, I., et al.: Simulations of Spectral Polarimetric Variables measured in rain at W-band, EGUsphere [preprint], https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2024-3164, 2025.
Re-used

Use of Standard File Formats: The DMP specifies that data will be provided in commonly used, non-proprietary formats such as NetCDF (Network Common Data Form) and ASCII. NetCDF is a community-endorsed standard in Earth sciences, supported by the Unidata community, which ensures platform independence and self-describing capabilities. This practice, recommended by organizations like the Research Data Alliance (RDA), facilitates data exchange across diverse systems and is widely adopted by projects like ACTRIS.

Adoption of Controlled Vocabularies: The DMP mandates the use of Climate and Forecast (CF) conventions, for the nomenclature of atmospheric variables (e.g., backscatter, extinction coefficients). The CF conventions, endorsed by the climate and atmospheric science communities, provide a standardized vocabulary that ensures interoperability across datasets like PollyXT lidar and Aeolus products. Additionally, EVDC metadata requirements, based on GEOMS, further align with community standards for Earth observation data.

Metadata Standards and Schemas: The project employs standardized metadata schemas, including Dublin Core (for Zenodo), CF conventions, and GEOMS (for EVDC). These schemas, endorsed by the research community and listed in the Metadata Standards Catalog, ensure that metadata are machine-readable and interoperable. The inclusion of detailed metadata (e.g., authors, keywords) supports discovery and integration with other datasets, a best practice promoted by FAIR data initiatives.

Interoperability with Community Repositories: Data deposition in Zenodo and EVDC follows community-endorsed practices, as both repositories support OAI-PMH for metadata harvesting and assign DOIs, aligning with standards set by DataCite and the OpenAIRE community. This ensures seamless integration with global research infrastructures like ACTRIS and Copernicus.

Quality Assurance Frameworks: The DMP adheres to ACTRIS recommendations, which are community-endorsed best practices. They promote standardize data collection, analysis, and distribution protocols, including measurement uncertainty and traceability, enhancing interoperability with datasets from other validated sources.

Collaboration and Data Integration: The project’s strategic collaborations with experienced partners (e.g., ESA, NASA, ACTRIS)  follow community best practices by combining diverse datasets (e.g., ASKOS) into a cohesive framework, a practice endorsed by the Group on Earth Observations (GEO) for interoperable data systems.

Searchable metadata are provided through registry/catalogue indexing:

Metadata Repository:
All data and their respective metadata will be deposited in trusted open-access repositories (e.g. EVDC and zenodo). These repositories function as metadata repositories, storing and indexing metadata in machine-readable formats to enable searchability. 

Registry/Catalogue:
A dedicated tab on the P4CV website (https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/, under Material->Datasets) that will summarize datasets with respective links. This tab serves as a registry or catalogue and acting as a searchable entry point.

Encryption: The utilization of trusted repositories such as Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) guarantees that data transmission and storage are secured through encryption, adhering to industry-standard practices for robust data handling and protection across these platforms.

Firewalls: To ensure the preservation of datasets, storage on NOA servers is complemented by the implementation of firewalls as an integral component of the NOA institutional security infrastructure, effectively safeguarding sensitive data against unauthorized access.
Use of Institutional Infrastructure: The primary cost coverage comes from the use of institutional infrastructure provided by the National Observatory of Athens (NOA), which hosts the P4CV project.

The data are being generated or reused in the P4CV project for multiple purposes: to obtain information, to share info, to develop or improve a product, and to combine with other data. Below is a detailed explanation of each purpose, reflecting the project’s scope and objectives:

To Obtain Information: gather detailed insights into atmospheric composition, cloud dynamics, and radiation for training activities (WP2, WP3) and calibration/validation (Cal/Val) studies (WP4). This foundational information is critical to understanding complex atmospheric processes and supporting the project’s research goals.

To Share Info: sharing data with a wide audience, including space agencies (e.g., ESA), academia, ESFRI projects (e.g., ACTRIS), Copernicus, regional stakeholders, industry, and society. The dedicated P4CV website tab (https://pangea4calval.space.noa.gr/materials_datasets_algorithms.php) further facilitates dissemination, enhancing the project’s impact.

To Develop a Product: the simulated scattering properties of rain particles in Tsikoudi et al. (2025), consist a new scattering database for rain particles.

To Improve a Product: use ground-based remote sensing data to improve Earth observation products via Cal/Val. 

To Combine with Other Data: The project integrates diverse datasets, such as ground-based data from ASKOS ESA Cal/Val campaign and ground-based remote sensing data acquired at PANGEA observatory, with satellite data from ESA, to create a comprehensive dataset.

textListValues.textListValue: Use of tools for automatic checks
Consistency verified with data models and standards

field.textValue:
Use of Tools for Automatic Checks: Use of standardized automatic analysis software developed within ACTRIS to process aerosol and cloud remote sensing datasets (e.g., PollyXT lidar profiles). This software performs automatic checks to remove duplicates, inconsistencies, and errors, ensuring data quality during processing.

Data Conform to Format Specification: Data provided in commonly used, non-proprietary formats such as NetCDF and ASCII, which are community-endorsed standards. This conformity ensures data are well-structured and compatible with analysis tools, facilitating interoperability and quality assurance.

Consistency Verified with Data Models and Standards: The project adheres to ACTRIS and QA4EO quality assurance frameworks, which include comparisons with data models and standards (e.g., CF conventions for variable naming). This verifies consistency across datasets, aligning with established protocols for accuracy and reliability.
Leads the Project Coordination Team and oversees the overall data management strategy.
Co-leads the Project Coordination Team, supporting data management and stewardship.
https://help.zenodo.org/docs/deposit/describe-records/keywords-and-subjects/

EVDC Metadata Requirements (aligned with CF Conventions and GEOMS Standard):
URL: https://evdc.esa.int/documentation
This vocabulary standardizes terms for satellite validation data (e.g., Aeolus L2A products) and correlative datasets, facilitating exchange and discovery. The EVDC documentation provides details on these standardized terms, tailored for EO and Cal/Val studies.
https://evdc.esa.int/documentation

The Pangea4CalVal (P4CV) project ensures that data provenance, or data lineage, is well documented as a critical component of its metadata, aligning with the FAIR principles and enhancing the dataset’s trustworthiness and reusability. This is supported by the following details:

Metadata Inclusion: The metadata for all data deposited in Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) will include information about provenance. This encompasses details such as the origin of the data (e.g., PANGEA observatory, ASKOS campaign, ESA missions), the date of deposit, and the authors or organizations involved (e.g., NOA, ESA), providing a clear history of the dataset’s creation and handling.

Data Origin Tracking: The DMP identifies specific sources of data, such as ground-based remote sensing data from the PANGEA observatory (e.g., PollyXT lidar profiles), satellite data from ESA repositories (e.g., Aeolus L2A products), and simulated scenes. For instance, the ASKOS campaign data is linked to a DOI (10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde), which includes provenance details in its metadata, traceable via the EVDC repository. This documentation tracks the data’s lineage from collection to deposition.

Standardized Documentation: The use of standardized vocabularies like CF conventions ensures that provenance is consistently recorded, including relationships between variables (e.g., linking ground-based lidar data with satellite products).

Standardised vocabularies are employed to enhance interoperability across systems, aligning with FAIR principles. Here’s the supporting evidence:

EVDC Metadata Requirements: Data deposited in the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) will adhere to EVDC’s naming conventions and metadata requirements, which are tailored for Earth observation and validation data. This discipline-specific standardization facilitates exchange with repositories and users like ESA and ACTRIS collaborators.

Keyword Standardization: The DMP lists specific keywords (e.g., Remote sensing, Ground-based, Satellite, Simulation, Cal/Val, Lidar, Radar, Sun-photometer, Radiation) to be included in metadata. These are selected to reflect standardized terms relevant to the project’s focus, enhancing discoverability and interoperability across systems.
https://github.com/jleinonen/pytmatrix/wiki
Deposition in Trusted Repositories
Digital
https://evdc.esa.int/
EVDC (ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center)

EVDC is repository, managed by ESA, is designed for atmospheric validation data and is linked to PANGEA through collaboration with ACTRIS central data facilities. It archives correlative data (e.g., Aeolus products) with DOIs, supporting Cal/Val research (WP4). EVDC ensures open access consistent with ESA policies, with metadata available for any restricted data.

Metadata are designed to enhance findability and support access to the dataset, aligning with FAIR principles. Here’s how this is achieved:

Access Information in Metadata: The DMP specifies that metadata for all data deposited in Zenodo and the ESA Atmospheric Validation Data Center (EVDC) will include key details to facilitate access. This includes persistent identifiers (PIDs) like Digital Object Identifiers (DOIs) (e.g., DOI: 10.60621/jatac.campaign.2021.2022.caboverde for ASKOS data), which serve as direct links to the dataset’s location in the repositories. The metadata also includes licensing terms (e.g., CC BY 4.0), indicating access rights, and repository URLs, guiding users to the download interface.
